header.subtitle: Systems Design Police Dept
menu.title: Menu
guide.title: Concept Guide
home.heroSubtitle: The city of Distributia runs on distributed systems. Things keep breaking. Diagnose failures. Design solutions. Solve cases.
chaos.sectionSubtitle: Inject failures, watch the system wobble, then restore stability with targeted fixes.
chaos.title: Chaos Simulator
chaos.subtitle: Introduce faults, observe behavior, then apply fixes to restore service health.
chaos.logsEmpty: No events yet. Activate a fault to begin.
chaos.preset.apiDbCache.name: API + DB + Cache
chaos.preset.apiDbCache.desc: A classic read-heavy stack with cache acceleration and a primary/replica database.
chaos.preset.eventOrders.name: Event-Driven Orders
chaos.preset.eventOrders.desc: Checkout sends work through a queue to background workers and notifications.
chaos.preset.realtimeAnalytics.name: Real-Time Analytics
chaos.preset.realtimeAnalytics.desc: Stream processing feeds a time-series store for dashboards.
chaos.fault.latencySpike.name: Latency Spike
chaos.fault.latencySpike.desc: Upstream calls exceed the p95 budget and slow the critical path.
chaos.fault.packetLoss.name: Packet Loss
chaos.fault.packetLoss.desc: Network drops cause retransmits and timeouts across services.
chaos.fault.nodeCrash.name: Node Crash
chaos.fault.nodeCrash.desc: A core service instance stops responding abruptly.
chaos.fault.dbPrimaryFail.name: DB Primary Failure
chaos.fault.dbPrimaryFail.desc: The primary database goes dark and writes stall.
chaos.fault.replicaLag.name: Replica Lag
chaos.fault.replicaLag.desc: Replicas fall behind, serving stale or inconsistent reads.
chaos.fault.cacheMissStorm.name: Cache Miss Storm
chaos.fault.cacheMissStorm.desc: Hot keys miss, forcing expensive origin reads.
chaos.fault.queueBacklog.name: Queue Backlog
chaos.fault.queueBacklog.desc: Queued work piles up faster than consumers can drain it.
chaos.fix.addRetries.name: Add Retries
chaos.fix.addRetries.desc: Retry transient failures with backoff to reduce error spikes.
chaos.fix.circuitBreaker.name: Enable Circuit Breaker
chaos.fix.circuitBreaker.desc: Fail fast and fall back when a dependency is unhealthy.
chaos.fix.replicaFailover.name: Replica Failover
chaos.fix.replicaFailover.desc: Promote a replica or shift reads to healthy nodes.
chaos.fix.scaleOut.name: Scale Out
chaos.fix.scaleOut.desc: Add capacity to absorb load and reduce queueing.
chaos.fix.cacheWarmup.name: Cache Warmup
chaos.fix.cacheWarmup.desc: Preload hot keys to recover hit rate quickly.
chaos.fix.queueAutoscale.name: Queue Autoscale
chaos.fix.queueAutoscale.desc: Increase consumers to drain backlog faster.

tutorial.step1Body: The symptoms are your first clues. Every case starts with a briefing — read it carefully before touching the diagram.
tutorial.step2Body: Tap the glowing components in the diagram to inspect their logs and evidence. That's where the real clues are.
tutorial.step3Body: Once you know what broke, diagnose the root cause and prescribe a fix in the panel on the right.
daily.rule2: Timed — the clock starts when you begin
daily.rule3: The case is revealed when you start

interview.sectionTitle: Think You're Ready? Prove It Under the Clock.
interview.sectionSubtitle: Three timed rounds, one attempt per question, then a postmortem writeup — the closest thing to the real interview.
interview.setup.title: Mock Interview
interview.setup.subtitle: A 25-minute simulated incident interview: three timed rounds, one attempt each, then a postmortem writeup with self-assessment.
interview.setup.lowRepsWarning: Detectives usually train before the exam.
interview.setup.abandonNotice: Leaving mid-interview ends the session — nothing partial is saved.
interview.round.label: Round
interview.postmortem.prompt: Write the incident summary: what failed, why, and what you'd change.
interview.postmortem.placeholder: What failed, why, and what you'd change...
interview.postmortem.rubricSubtitle: Score yourself honestly — these don't count toward your total.
interview.rubric.q1: State the root cause — not a symptom.
interview.rubric.q2: Name the failing component and the propagation path.
interview.rubric.q3: Propose a fix that removes the cause class, not just the instance.
interview.rubric.q4: Mention a detection or prevention mechanism (alert, replica, circuit breaker…).
interview.rubric.q5: Note a trade-off of your fix.
interview.rubric.replication.1: e.g. 'The primary's replication lag exceeded the failover threshold, not just that reads were stale.'
interview.rubric.replication.2: e.g. 'The stale replica served reads because the load balancer didn't check replication lag before routing.'
interview.rubric.replication.3: e.g. 'Enforce a max staleness bound on replicas instead of manually promoting the one replica that lagged.'
interview.rubric.replication.4: e.g. 'A replication-lag alert on each replica, paired with automatic removal from the read pool.'
interview.rubric.replication.5: e.g. 'Stricter staleness bounds reduce read throughput during network partitions.'
interview.rubric.consistency.1: e.g. 'A network partition split the quorum, not that one node "went down".'
interview.rubric.consistency.2: e.g. 'Two nodes formed a minority partition and kept accepting writes without a majority.'
interview.rubric.consistency.3: e.g. 'Require a majority quorum for every write, not just retry the specific failed request.'
interview.rubric.consistency.4: e.g. 'A split-brain alert when a node loses quorum, paired with fencing before it accepts writes.'
interview.rubric.consistency.5: e.g. 'Stronger quorum requirements add write latency and reduce availability during partitions.'
interview.rubric.loadBalancing.1: e.g. 'One backend was overloaded because the balancer ignored per-node latency, not that "traffic was high".'
interview.rubric.loadBalancing.2: e.g. 'The round-robin balancer kept sending requests to a saturated node instead of routing around it.'
interview.rubric.loadBalancing.3: e.g. 'Switch to least-connections or latency-aware routing, not just add one more server.'
interview.rubric.loadBalancing.4: e.g. 'Per-backend latency/error alerts feeding the balancer's health checks.'
interview.rubric.loadBalancing.5: e.g. 'Latency-aware routing costs extra bookkeeping and can flap under noisy metrics.'
interview.rubric.caching.1: e.g. 'A stampede of cache misses hit the database at once, not that the cache was "slow".'
interview.rubric.caching.2: e.g. 'The cache expired simultaneously for a hot key, and every request fell through to the DB.'
interview.rubric.caching.3: e.g. 'Add jittered TTLs or request coalescing, not just increase the cache size.'
interview.rubric.caching.4: e.g. 'A cache hit-rate alert plus a circuit breaker that limits concurrent DB fallthrough.'
interview.rubric.caching.5: e.g. 'Jittered TTLs slightly increase average staleness in exchange for avoiding thundering herds.'
interview.rubric.messaging.1: e.g. 'A slow consumer backed up the queue until producers started blocking, not that "messages were lost".'
interview.rubric.messaging.2: e.g. 'One consumer instance stalled on a poison message, halting the whole partition behind it.'
interview.rubric.messaging.3: e.g. 'Add a dead-letter queue and per-message timeout, not just restart the consumer.'
interview.rubric.messaging.4: e.g. 'Consumer-lag alerting per partition, plus a circuit breaker to shed load before backpressure cascades.'
interview.rubric.messaging.5: e.g. 'Dead-lettering risks silently dropping messages that need manual reprocessing.'
interview.rubric.storage.1: e.g. 'A shard's disk filled up because of skewed key distribution, not that "storage ran out".'
interview.rubric.storage.2: e.g. 'Hot writes concentrated on one shard, exhausting its disk while others sat mostly idle.'
interview.rubric.storage.3: e.g. 'Re-shard using a better hash/partition key, not just provision more disk on the hot shard.'
interview.rubric.storage.4: e.g. 'Per-shard disk-usage and write-skew alerts before any shard nears capacity.'
interview.rubric.storage.5: e.g. 'Re-sharding is disruptive and needs a migration window with temporary double-writes.'
interview.rubric.network.1: e.g. 'A DNS TTL mismatch kept routing traffic to a decommissioned node, not that "the network was flaky".'
interview.rubric.network.2: e.g. 'Clients cached a stale DNS record and kept connecting to an IP that no longer served the service.'
interview.rubric.network.3: e.g. 'Lower the DNS TTL and add client-side retry with re-resolution, not just restart the old node.'
interview.rubric.network.4: e.g. 'Connection-error-rate alerts per endpoint, plus health checks that catch stale routing early.'
interview.rubric.network.5: e.g. 'Very low TTLs increase DNS query volume and load on resolvers.'
interview.rubric.advanced.1: e.g. 'A distributed lock wasn't released after a client crash, not that the resource was "stuck".'
interview.rubric.advanced.2: e.g. 'The lock holder crashed mid-transaction and no lease expiry reclaimed the lock.'
interview.rubric.advanced.3: e.g. 'Use a lease with a TTL and fencing tokens, not just manually clear the lock.'
interview.rubric.advanced.4: e.g. 'Lock-hold-duration alerting, plus automatic lease expiry as a safety net.'
interview.rubric.advanced.5: e.g. 'Fencing tokens add complexity to every write path that touches the locked resource.'

cheatsheet.sectionSubtitle: Pacing, core concepts, magic phrases, and trade-offs for the 45-minute design round.
cheatsheet.title: System Design Cheat Sheet
cheatsheet.subtitle: The minimum viable reference for the 45-minute system design round. Memorize the rhythm, name the trade-offs, and avoid the unforced errors.
cheatsheet.structureSubtitle: If you burn 20 minutes on requirements and rush the deep dive, you fail even if you "know" the answer. Pacing is the skill.
cheatsheet.phase.0.title: Requirements
cheatsheet.phase.0.points.0: Functional: what does the system DO?
cheatsheet.phase.0.points.2: Scale: DAU, data volume, read:write ratio
cheatsheet.phase.0.points.3: Out-of-scope: force the interviewer to commit
cheatsheet.phase.1.title: Capacity Estimation
cheatsheet.phase.2.title: API / Data Model
cheatsheet.phase.2.points.2: NoSQL for scale + known access patterns
cheatsheet.phase.3.title: High-Level Design
cheatsheet.phase.3.points.0: Client → LB → API GW → Services → DB + Cache + Queue
cheatsheet.phase.4.title: Deep Dive
cheatsheet.phase.4.points.0: Pick ONE component and walk a request end-to-end
cheatsheet.phase.4.points.2: Justify every choice with a trade-off
cheatsheet.phase.4.points.3: This is where most of the signal lives
cheatsheet.phase.5.title: Scale & Bottlenecks
cheatsheet.phase.6.title: Closing
cheatsheet.phase.6.points.1: Mention what you'd do with more time
cheatsheet.phase.6.points.2: Acknowledge what you're NOT optimizing for
cheatsheet.conceptsSubtitle: About 14 ideas cover 80% of system-design interviews. These are the ones that show up again and again in the precinct's case files.
cheatsheet.concept.0.title: Load Balancing
cheatsheet.concept.0.summary: L4 (TCP) vs L7 (HTTP). Round-robin vs least-connections vs consistent-hash. Consistent hash is your answer when sticky routing matters.
cheatsheet.concept.1.title: Caching
cheatsheet.concept.1.summary: Placement: client → CDN → reverse proxy → app memory → Redis → DB. Policies: LRU, LFU, TTL. Mitigate stampedes with request coalescing or staggered TTLs.
cheatsheet.concept.2.title: SQL vs NoSQL
cheatsheet.concept.2.summary: Default to Postgres unless you have a reason. SQL for transactions, joins, strong consistency. NoSQL for massive writes, denormalized data, known access patterns.
cheatsheet.concept.3.title: Sharding / Partitioning
cheatsheet.concept.3.summary: Range-based is simple but can hotspot. Hash-based spreads load but hurts range queries. Consistent hashing with virtual nodes for elastic clusters.
cheatsheet.concept.4.title: Replication & Consistency
cheatsheet.concept.4.summary: Leader-follower, multi-leader, synchronous vs async. Read-your-own-writes often means routing to the leader or a sticky session.
cheatsheet.concept.5.title: CAP Theorem
cheatsheet.concept.5.summary: Under partition, pick Consistency OR Availability. CP systems reject writes to avoid split-brain; AP systems accept writes and reconcile later.
cheatsheet.concept.6.title: Message Queues
cheatsheet.concept.6.summary: Kafka for logs and replay. RabbitMQ/SQS for task queues. Pub/Sub for fan-out. Exactly-once = idempotency + dedup on the consumer.
cheatsheet.concept.7.title: CDNs
cheatsheet.concept.7.summary: Mention for anything user-facing. Static assets, images, video. Cache busting via content-hashed filenames. Dynamic content via signed URLs or edge caching.
cheatsheet.concept.8.title: Rate Limiting
cheatsheet.concept.8.summary: Token bucket for bursts, leaky bucket for smoothing, fixed window for simplicity, sliding window for accuracy. Distributed: Redis + atomic ops.
cheatsheet.concept.9.title: Consistent Hashing
cheatsheet.concept.9.summary: Adding/removing a node affects ~1/N keys. Virtual nodes smooth imbalance. Used in DynamoDB, Cassandra, Memcached clients, CDN routing.
cheatsheet.concept.10.title: Real-Time Transport
cheatsheet.concept.10.summary: Long polling for simple proxies, SSE for server-to-client text streams, WebSockets for full-duplex low-latency (chat, games, collaboration).
cheatsheet.concept.11.title: Search
cheatsheet.concept.11.summary: Elasticsearch/OpenSearch for full-text, autocomplete, facets. It's an inverted index, not a system of record — keep one alongside.
cheatsheet.concept.12.title: AuthN / AuthZ
cheatsheet.concept.12.summary: Session-based (stateful, cookie-in-Redis) vs JWT (stateless). OAuth 2.0 flows. RBAC vs ABAC for multi-tenant permissions.
cheatsheet.magicPhrasesSubtitle: Use these with intent. They are senior-level signals when backed by a trade-off, buzzwords when they aren't.
cheatsheet.magicPhrase.0: "This has a read-to-write ratio of roughly 100:1, so I'm optimizing for reads."
cheatsheet.magicPhrase.1: "I'd denormalize here to avoid joins at query time, at the cost of an extra write path."
cheatsheet.magicPhrase.2: "Eventual consistency is acceptable here, which lets us scale horizontally."
cheatsheet.magicPhrase.3: "This is a classic thundering-herd problem; I'd solve it with request coalescing."
cheatsheet.magicPhrase.4: "This operation should be idempotent so we can safely retry."
cheatsheet.magicPhrase.5: "Given the hot-partition risk, I'd shard on a composite key."
cheatsheet.magicPhrase.6: "I'd use a write-ahead log here for durability before the commit."
cheatsheet.magicPhrase.7: "I'd put a bloom filter in front of the DB to avoid hitting it for keys that don't exist."
cheatsheet.juniorMistake.0.title: Jumping to architecture before clarifying requirements
cheatsheet.juniorMistake.0.body: "Design Twitter" is not a spec. Ask about scale and scope first, or you'll optimize for the wrong thing.
cheatsheet.juniorMistake.1.title: Not back-of-the-envelope'ing
cheatsheet.juniorMistake.1.body: "Millions of users" means nothing. "100M DAU × 3 tweets/day = 3,500 writes/sec average, 35,000 peak" builds credibility in 60 seconds.
cheatsheet.juniorMistake.2.title: Saying "I'd use Redis" instead of "I'd use Redis because…"
cheatsheet.juniorMistake.2.body: The answer is never the component; it's the trade-off. Name the property you need (single-digit-ms reads, TTL invalidation, horizontal scale) and why that component provides it.
cheatsheet.onePageSubtitle: The distillation. Read it the night before, glance at it before the deep dive.
cheatsheet.onePageBlock:
REQUIREMENTS       (5 min — force scope)
  • Functional: "What does it DO?"
  • Non-functional: latency, QPS, consistency, availability
  • Scale: DAU, data volume, read:write ratio

CAPACITY ESTIMATION  (3–5 min — build credibility)
  • DAU × requests/user = QPS
  • QPS × avg payload = bandwidth
  • QPS × seconds × retention = storage
  • Cache ≈ 20% of hot data

HIGH-LEVEL DESIGN    (10 min — boxes + arrows)
  Client → LB → API GW → Services → DB + Cache + Queue
          CDN → Static Assets
          WebSocket / SSE for real-time

DATA MODEL           (5 min)
  • Entities + relationships
  • SQL for relational + transactions
  • NoSQL for scale + known access patterns
  • Denormalize when read-heavy

DEEP DIVE            (15 min — pick ONE component)
  • Walk through a user action end-to-end
  • Name specific tech (Kafka, Redis, Postgres)
  • Justify every choice with a trade-off

SCALE & BOTTLENECKS  (10 min — senior signal)
  • "What breaks at 10x traffic?"
  • Sharding strategy
  • Replication + consistency
  • Rate limiting + backpressure

CLOSING              (2 min)
  • Recap the key trade-offs
  • Mention what you'd do with more time
  • Acknowledge what you're NOT optimizing for
